title: LAUSD Votes to Slash Screen Time, Joining a Growing State-Level Movement
summary: When Lila Byock's 11-year-old son was penalized for not having his school-issued iPad during PE class, she decided she'd had enough. "There was no justification for why it was better," she said of the digital learning that persisted long after pandemic remote schooling ended. "It was just sort of, 'Well, we got these things during COVID and might as well keep using them.'" Byock channeled her frustration into action, co-founding Schools Beyond Screens, an advocacy group that now counts thousands of parents across the United States. Last week, their months of petitions and demonstrations paid off: the Los Angeles Unified School District (LAUSD) board voted unanimously to limit screen time for all grade levels starting this fall, with a particular focus on eliminating it entirely for elementary-age students. The move is a dramatic reversal for a district that, since the pandemic, had aggressively pushed technology into classrooms. It also aligns with a flurry of recent state legislation. Since January, Alabama, Tennessee, Utah, and Virginia have passed laws to reevaluate technology's role in education, and more than 10 other states are considering similar restrictions. T. Philip Nichols, an associate professor at Baylor University who studies technology in public education, calls the LAUSD vote "the pendulum swing" — a shocking but welcome shift. His research argues that widespread computer use hasn't delivered on promises of higher test scores. "These platforms are also gathering data about how students are participating in them so that they can sell products back to schools," Nichols warned. "When you are reading a textbook, that textbook is not reading you back." Not everyone agrees a broad ban is wise. Tracy Weeks of the ed-tech company Instructure cautions against "throwing the baby out with the bathwater," noting that interactive learning activities differ vastly from passive scrolling. LAUSD administrators now have a June deadline to craft an official policy, with rollout expected this fall. In Utah, a back-to-basics law takes effect July 1, though classroom enforcement timelines remain unclear. "We're not going to get this exactly right on the first try, but we're certainly moving in the right direction," said Utah Governor Spencer Cox. Missouri's House also passed a bipartisan screen-time bill this spring, now headed to the state Senate.
why_it_matters: As schools across the country reconsider technology's role, today's students are on the front lines of a massive educational experiment that will shape how they learn, socialize, and even have their personal data protected for years to come.

keywords.explanation: When everyone on a group votes the same way, with no one disagreeing.
A group of people who work together to push for a certain cause or change.
A sharp reversal from one extreme to another, like a pendulum moving back and forth.
Short for educational technology, meaning digital tools and platforms used in schools.
Supported by members of two major political parties, often showing wide agreement.
A return to simpler, traditional methods, focusing on core fundamentals instead of new approaches.

questions.question: What did the LAUSD board decide to do regarding screen time?
options: A. Increase screen time for all grades
B. Limit screen time starting this fall
C. Ban iPads entirely in the district
D. Delay the decision until next year
questions.correct_answer: B. Limit screen time starting this fall

questions.question: Which states passed laws to reevaluate technology in education, according to the article?
options: A. California, Texas, Florida, New York
B. Alabama, Tennessee, Utah, Virginia
C. Missouri, Kansas, Nebraska, Iowa
D. Georgia, North Carolina, Ohio, Michigan

questions.question: What concern does T. Philip Nichols raise about educational technology platforms?
options: A. They improve test scores dramatically
B. They gather student data to sell products back to schools
C. They are too expensive for most districts
D. They replace teachers entirely
questions.correct_answer: B. They gather student data to sell products back to schools

questions.question: What warning does Tracy Weeks of Instructure give?
options: A. That technology should be banned completely
B. That schools must adopt only textbooks
C. Not to throw the baby out with the bathwater
D. That LAUSD should ignore the vote
questions.correct_answer: C. Not to throw the baby out with the bathwater

questions.question: When will LAUSD administrators need to craft an official policy?
options: A. By the end of the year
B. By June
C. By next spring
D. Immediately

questions.question: Which state's governor said, 'We're not going to get this exactly right on the first try, but we're certainly moving in the right direction'?
options: A. California
B. Utah
C. Missouri
D. Alabama

background_read: During the COVID-19 pandemic, school districts across the United States, including LAUSD (the second-largest U.S. school district, serving over 600,000 students in Los Angeles), rapidly distributed millions of iPads and Chromebooks to enable remote learning. Once schools reopened, many kept those devices in daily use without re-evaluating their effects.

Similar debates have unfolded globally: in 2018, France banned smartphones in primary and middle schools to protect attention and social interaction. In the U.S., California passed laws on student data privacy as early as 2014, reflecting long-standing worries that educational apps might harvest personal information.

T. Philip Nichols, an associate professor at Baylor University who studies technology in public education, has argued that the promised boost in test scores never materialized. Tracy Weeks is a spokesperson for Instructure, a company best known for the Canvas learning management system used by many colleges and schools.

Utah Governor Spencer Cox (a Republican first elected in 2020) signed a back-to-basics law that takes effect July 1, signalling a broader shift away from classroom technology that many parents and lawmakers see as having gone too far.
Article_Structure: LEAD: A mother's frustration over her son's iPad use in PE class
└─ PERSONAL TURNING POINT: Lila Byock's reaction turns into activism
ACTION: The LAUSD board votes unanimously to limit screen time
└─ DISTRICT REVERSAL: Shift from aggressively pushing tech since the pandemic
NATIONAL TREND: Multiple states pass laws to reevaluate classroom technology
├─ SPECIFIC STATES: Alabama, Tennessee, Utah, Virginia, and more than 10 others
└─ MISSOURI: Bipartisan screen-time bill headed to state Senate
EXPERT VOICE: T. Philip Nichols calls the vote a 'pendulum swing' and warns about data collection
COUNTERPOINT: Ed-tech company Instructure cautions against a blanket ban
IMPLEMENTATION: LAUSD sets June deadline for official policy, Utah's law takes effect July 1
CLOSING QUOTE: Governor Cox acknowledges the effort may not be perfect but is a step forward

perspectives.description: Parents like Lila Byock and groups like Schools Beyond Screens believe limiting screen time will refocus classrooms on direct instruction and social interaction, protecting children's attention spans and privacy. They see the unanimous LAUSD vote as a powerful victory for grassroots activism.

perspectives.perspective: Negative / critical (ed-tech industry and some educators)
perspectives.description: Companies such as Instructure, represented by Tracy Weeks, warn that a blanket ban ignores the benefits of interactive learning apps that differ greatly from passive scrolling. They argue that discarding all technology could throw away valuable tools that personalize education and engage students.

Scholars like T. Philip Nichols note that evidence on technology's educational impact is mixed and that the pandemic forced a rapid, untested adoption of devices. The current pushback may be a natural correction after years of unchecked digital expansion, yet the exact balance remains unclear.

The LAUSD policy, once drafted, will serve as a test case for districts nationwide. If it improves student well-being without hurting learning, many other states will likely follow; if it proves too restrictive, a new compromise may emerge that selectively uses technology for specific purposes.
